perturbation.original_text: The Sponsor will own all right, title and interes t in and to the Project IP other than copyright in Publications as that term is defined i n the following section. The Sponsor grants each of McGill and the Intern, and if applicable the Aca demic Supervisor and members of the research team shall forever retain a royalty-free, non-exclu sive, perpetual, irrevocable license to use the Project IP for research, scholarly publication, edu cational and other non-commercial purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sponsor will own all right, title and interes t in and to the Project IP other than copyright in Publications as that term is defined i n the following section. The Sponsor grants each of McGill and the Intern a *revocable* license to use the Project IP for research, scholarly publication, edu cational and other non-commercial purposes. This license can be terminated by the Sponsor with 30 days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The change from a 'perpetual, irrevocable license' to a 'revocable license' directly contradicts the original meaning of the agreement. It creates uncertainty regarding the extent to which McGill and the Intern can utilize the project IP for academic purposes, as the Sponsor now possesses the power to revoke this license. The 'perpetual, irrevocable license' is in section 3.2, by making it revocable it allows the sponsor to terminate the license with a 30 day written notice, this introduces an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: PROJECT IP : The Sponsor will own all right, title and interes t in and to the Project IP other than copyright in Publications as that term is defined i n the following section. The Sponsor grants each of McGill and the Intern a *revocable* license to use the Project IP for research, scholarly publication, edu cational and other non-commercial purposes. This license can be terminated by the Sponsor with 30 days written notice.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 'perpetual, irrevocable license,' while the changed text modifies this to a 'revocable license' that can be terminated with 30 days' notice. The contradicted text states 'revocable license', creating a direct and clear conflict. This change allows the Sponsor to revoke the license, which directly contradicts the previous, non-revocable agreement. The change creates a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient must keep Confidential Information in confidence. Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information only to such of its employ ees, directors, officers, agents, consultants and administrators who have a need-to-know such informa tion for the Project provided that they are advised of the confidential nature of the Confident ial Information and are under an obligation to maintain its confidentiality. Recipient must not ot herwise disclose Confidential Information to any person or third party without the prior written app roval of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient must keep Confidential Information in confidence. Recipient *is encouraged, but not required, to* disclose the Confidential Information to such of its employ ees, directors, officers, agents, consultants and administrators who have a need-to-know such informa tion for the Project provided that they are advised of the confidential nature of the Confident ial Information and are under an obligation to maintain its confidentiality. Recipient *may* otherwise disclose Confidential Information to any person or third party *even* without the prior written app roval of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires strict confidentiality, permitting disclosure only to those with a 'need-to-know' and under confidentiality obligations. The modified text contradicts this by encouraging disclosure and removing the requirement for prior written approval for disclosing to other parties. This creates significant ambiguity regarding confidentiality obligations and weakens the protection of confidential information. This in-text contradiction, introduces uncertainty in section 7.1 of the document
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient must keep Confidential Information in confidence. Recipient *is encouraged, but not required, to* disclose the Confidential Information to such of its employ ees, directors, officers, agents, consultants and administrators who have a need-to-know such informa tion for the Project provided that they are advised of the confidential nature of the Confident ial Information and are under an obligation to maintain its confidentiality. Recipient *may* otherwise disclose Confidential Information to any person or third party *even* without the prior written app roval of the Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly alters the original obligation of confidentiality. The original text mandates keeping Confidential Information in confidence and restricts disclosure to only those with a 'need-to-know' under confidentiality obligations, requiring prior written approval for other disclosures. The changed text contradicts this by 'encouraging' disclosure and removing the need for prior approval, directly conflicting with the original intent of confidentiality. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, but the explanation highlights the deviation from the original text and how it weakens confidentiality, thereby creating a contradiction. The change directly violates the original confidentiality obligation, leading to uncertainty and potential legal disputes. This constitutes a strong, in-text contradiction, which would cause confusion over which terms should be enforced.
